title: The Tracking-by-Detection Paradigm
concept: Tracking-by-detection is the dominant MOT approach: run an object detector on every frame independently, then link detections across frames into consistent tracks using motion and appearance cues.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does a 'track ID' in Multi-Object Tracking represent?

options.text: A consistent label that identifies the same object across multiple video frames
The pixel resolution of a bounding box
The confidence score of a single detection

exercises.explain: Yes! A track ID represents persistent object identity over time.

cards.title: Detect first, connect the dots second
cards.body: The most common way to build a tracker is surprisingly modular: don't try to solve detection and tracking together. Instead, detect objects in each frame separately, THEN write a second algorithm that connects those detections into tracks.

cards.title: Tracking-by-Detection
cards.body: Tracking-by-detection is a two-stage pipeline: (1) run an object detector (like Faster R-CNN or YOLO) independently on every frame to get per-frame boxes, then (2) run a separate data association algorithm that links boxes across consecutive frames into continuous tracks.

cards.title: Photographs plus a detective's notebook
cards.body: Stage 1 is like a photographer snapping individual photos of the party every second. Stage 2 is like a detective reviewing all those photos afterward, connecting the dots: 'this person in photo 5 is the same person circled in photo 4, based on position and appearance.'

cards.title: SORT and DeepSORT
cards.body: Classic tracking-by-detection algorithms like SORT (Simple Online and Realtime Tracking) and DeepSORT use a detector's output boxes each frame, then match them frame-to-frame using motion prediction (Kalman filters) and, in DeepSORT's case, learned appearance features.

cards.title: The detector doesn't know about tracking at all
cards.body: It's easy to assume the detector itself understands motion or identity. In tracking-by-detection, the detector is completely unaware of tracking — it just outputs fresh boxes each frame, with zero memory. All the identity logic lives entirely in the separate association stage.

left.text:
Runs independently per frame
No memory of past frames
Outputs fresh boxes each time

right.text:
Links boxes across frames
Uses motion + appearance cues
Produces final track IDs

exercises.prompt: What are the two stages of the tracking-by-detection paradigm?

options.text: Independent per-frame object detection, followed by a separate data association step linking boxes across frames
Training and then testing the same detector twice
Upscaling followed by downscaling the video

exercises.explain: Correct! Detection and association are two clearly separated stages.

exercises.prompt: True or false: In tracking-by-detection, the object detector itself has memory of previous frames.
exercises.explain: False. The detector runs independently each frame with zero memory; all identity logic lives in the separate association stage.

exercises.sentence: The second stage of tracking-by-detection, which links detections across frames, is called data ___.
exercises.explain: Data association connects per-frame detections into continuous tracks.

exercises.prompt: Which of these are examples of classic tracking-by-detection algorithms?
exercises.explain: Correct! SORT and DeepSORT are well-known tracking-by-detection algorithms.

exercises.prompt: A tracking-by-detection system's detector misses a pedestrian for exactly one frame (due to brief occlusion behind a pole), then re-detects them the next frame. What happens to their track ID by default?

options.text: Without a re-identification mechanism, the association stage may assign a brand-new track ID, breaking the continuity of that pedestrian's track
The detector automatically remembers the pedestrian and never loses the ID
The video freezes until the pedestrian reappears
All other tracked objects in the frame lose their IDs too

exercises.explain: Exactly! Missed detections can break track continuity — a core challenge we'll dig into next: data association matching.
